# MAIN ARTICLE Full Length Article # Co3O4mirobelts: Preparation with the electrospinning technique and its investigation in peroxidase-like activity Haiyan Sun ∗ , Weiyue Zhu Analytical and Testing Center, Qilu University of Technology, Jinan, Shandong250353, PR China # a r t i c l e i n f o Article history: Received19September2016Received in revised form3December2016Accepted9December2016Available online10December2016 Keywords: Co3O4Microbelts Elelectrospinning technique Peroxidase-like activity # 1. Intorduction Natural horseradish peroxidase (HRP) is an important hemecontaining enzyme that generally extracted from the plants, and shows high substrate specificity and high catalytic efficiency under mild conditions [1,2]. It has attracted considerable attention from a variety of disciplines for its practical and commercial applications. However, it has intrinsic limitations such as high costs for preparation and purification, low stability caused by denaturation, and limitation for large-scale applications [3]. Therefore, it is desirable to search of the peroxidase alternative with low cost, easy storage, and flexibility, as well as high substrate specificity and stability even under rigorous conditions. As an important transition-metal oxide, cobalt oxide (Co3O4), which owns a mixed-valency spinel structure is widely applied in gas sensors [4], lithium-ion batteries [5], supercapacitor [6], and catalysts for CO oxidation and hydrocarbon compound combustion [7]. Since Fe3O4nanoparticle was reported to possess an intrinsic peroxidase-like activity [8], great effort has been devoted to develop nanomaterial-based artificial enzymes, such as noble metal nanoparitlces [9-13], some metal oxides or sulfide [14-17], carbon-based materials [18-23], composites and so on [24-27]. Also, Co3O4nanomaterials such as nanoparticle and three dimentiional (3D) nanostructures were reported their catalytic activities to H2O2similar to those of Fe3O4nanoparticles [8], showing poten- ∗ Corresponding author. E-mail address: sunhy0120@gmail.com (H. Sun). # a b s t r a c t Co3O4microbelts were prepared with cobalt nitrate and PVP as raw materials by an electrospinning technique combined with subsequent calcination. The belt-like Co3O4was characterized by XRD, SEM, TEM, FT-IR and its N2adsorption-desorption behavior was measured. In addition, its peroxidase-like activity was investigated with H2O2and3,3 ′ ,5,5 ′ -tetramethylbenzidine as substrates, and the product with the highest specific area exhibits the best catalytic activity and stability. © 2016Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved. tial as enzyme mimics in H2O2reactions [28-33]. It is reported that the intrinsic peroxidase-like activity of Co3O4nanoparticles originated from their ability to transfer electron between substrates and H2O2 [29,30]. Jia et al. developed a platform to detect hydrogen peroxide and glucose by using the peroxidase-lke activity of Co3O4nanoparticles [33]. When Co3O4nanoparticles were served as enzyme mimics, they are several shortcomings, for instance, (1) the procedures to prepare nanoparticles with high quality are generally complicated; (2) intrinsic instability of nanomaterials can susceptibly cause the surface oxidation/aggregation which lead to the decrease and even loss of their catalytic activity; (3) some active sites on the surface of nanomaterials may be dampened, for the capping agents were often employed to prevent the nanoparticles from aggregation; (4) there are some difficulties in recycling of the nanoparticles. These drawbacks seriously limit their practical applications. Therefore, it is necessary to develop a simple method to prepare stable and easily recyclable Co3O4nanomaterials with high activity. Compared with zero dimentional nanostructure, one dimentional (1D) nanomaterial generally composed by small blocks will endow it3D open net-works, so it usually has many pores, high specific surface area and active sites. Besides, 1D structure can be easily recycled in the particle application. As is well-known, the electrospinning technique is a simple and efficient route to prepare1D nanostuctured materials, such as nanofibers, nanobelts and so on [34-36], so the electrospinning technique is usually employed to prepare1D nanostructured materials. Herein, 1D Co3O4microbelts were simply prepared with an electrospinning technique combined with calcination. The Contents lists available at ScienceDirect # Applied Surface Science j o u r n a l h om epa ge: www.elsevier.com/locate/apsusc peroxidase-like activity of the mircobelts was characterized, presenting superior activity and stability. # 2. Experiment section # 2.1. Synthesis In the typical synthesis of the sol, 1.0g polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP, Mw = 1,300,000) was dissolved in10mL of ethanol with magnetic stirring. When PVP dissolved completely, the aqueous solution prepared by dissolving0.45g of Co(NO3)2 · 6H2O in2mL of deionied water was added. After the mixture became homogeneous, it was transferred to a needle tube, and the inner diameter of the needle is0.5mm. The electrospinning voltage was set as20kV and the receiving distance between needle and collector was20cm while the pumping speed was0.002mm/s. After the electrospinning process, the mat-like fiber was collected and heated at80 ◦ C overnight. Finally, the dried gel fiber was heated to300 ◦ C in the tube furnace with heating rate of1 ◦ C/min and kept at300 ◦ C for1h and then heated to400 ◦ C with heating rate of0.5 ◦ C/min, being kept at that temperature for2h. # 2.2. Characterization The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of the powder samples were collected at room temperature on a Rigaku D/MAX2200PC diffractometer with a graphite monochromator and CuK 𝛂 ( 𝛌 =0.15418nm) radiation. The morphology and microstructure of the products were characterized using a transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEOL JEM-2100) and scanning electron microscope (SEM, FEI Quanta200). The infrared (IR) spectra were examined on a Nicolet5DX Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrometer using the KBr pellet technique. Nitrogen adsorptiondesorption data were recorded on a Quadrasorb SI apparatus at liquid nitrogen temperature (T = -196 ◦ C). Thermal gravimetric (TG) analysis was carried out from25 ◦ C to600 ◦ C at10.0 ◦ C/min under air atmosphere (Mettler Toledo, TGA/SDTA851e). Electron spin resonance (ESR) measurement was performed on a Bruker EMX ESR spectrometer (Billerica, MA) at room temperature with5,5 ′ -dimethyl-1-pyrrolline N-oxide (DMPO) as a spin trap. # 2.3. Peroxidase-like activity measurement The reaction was performed at40 ◦ C in3mL of acetate buffer (0.2M, pH = 4.0). First, 36 𝛍 g of catalyst was dispersed in above solution, and sonicated for5min. Then, 150 𝛍 L (6.2 𝛍 mol) of3,3 ′ ,5,5 ′ -tetramethylbenzidine (TMB, 10mg/mL, with dimethylsulfoxides as solvent) and160 𝛍 L (5.2 𝛍 mol) of H2O2 (30wt%) were added as substrates. After reacting for a certain time, the absorbance of TMB-derived oxidation product was examined at652nm on UV-vis spectrometer (Perkin-Elmer Lambda35). The peroxidase-like activity was evaluated by absorbance data. Michaelis constants (Km) were obtained by varying the concentrations of TMB and H2O2at the optimal conditions. # 3. Results and discussion In the synthesis of sol, PVP was added as spinning additive to improve its spinnability and the addition of ethanol can effectively increase the spinning speed. From the TG curve of the gel fiber (Fig. 1a), there are three weight losses. The first weight loss (<100 ◦ C, ca. 10%) is assigned to the removal of the absorbed water and ethanol. The second obvious step (ca. 55.5%) in the range from100 ◦ C to331 ◦ C is due to the decomposition of cobalt nitrate and the side chains in PVP. The last (ca. 25.5%) is ascribed to the D 6004000 3200 fiber gelfiber 2400 1600 800 Fig. 1. TG curve of gel fiber (a) and IR spectra of gel fiber and the sample after calcination (b). Fig. 2. XRD patterns of the obtained fiber. decomposition of the main chains in PVP. The weight of the gel fiber became constant when the heat temperature reached400 ◦ C, demonstrating that the organics could be removed completely at this temperature. From the IR spectrum of the gel fibers (Fig. 1b), the asymmetrical adsorption at3431cm -1is attributed to the stretching vibration of OH in the adsorbed water and ethanol. The adsorption peaks from3100cm -1to930cm -1are the characteristic vibration of PVP. The adsorption peaks at2949cm -1and1423cm -1are ascribed to the vibration of C H bonds. The vibration at1674cm -1is due to C O stretching vibration and that at1020cm -1is assigned to the stretching vibration of C N bonds in PVP [37]. The vibration at1384cm -1is the assigned to NO3 -[38]. After calcination at400 ◦ C, the characteristic IR adsorption of PVP disappeared, indicating PVP was removed completely after the heat treatment. The adsorption at664cm -1and571cm -1are the vibration of Co O bonds in Co (II, III) oxide [39]. The disappearance of vibration of NO3 -at1384cm -1demonstrates Co(NO3)2has converted to Co3O4 [40]. The crystal structure of the obtained product was determined by XRD. As shown in Fig. 2, all the diffraction peaks are matched well with those of cubic Co3O4 (JCPDS no.43-1003). The diffraction peak at31.3 ◦ , 36.9 ◦ , 44.9 ◦ , 59.4 ◦ , 65.3 ◦ are indexed to (220), (311), (400), (511) and (440) reflections. There are no diffraction peaks of other impurities, indicating the pure phase of the product. According to Scherrer line width analysis on (311) reflection, the crystalline size of the sample is estimated ca. 26.9 ± 1.1nm. SEM and TEM were used to characterize the morphology of the products, which are shown in Fig. 3. From Fig. 3a, the electrospun gel fibers present belt-like morphology with width of ca. 4.9 ± 1.5 𝛍 m and thickness of0.6 ± 02 𝛍 m. The surface of the belt-like gel fiber is quiet smooth due the amorphous nature of Fig. 3. SEM images of gel fibers (a) and the calcined fibers (b) and TEM (c) and HR-TEM image (d) of the calcined fiber. PVP/cobalt nitrate composites. After calcinitaion at400 ◦ C for2h (Fig. 3b), the belt-like morphology retained while the width of the belt reduced to1.9 ± 0.6 𝛍 m and the surface of the belts became coarse because of the decomposition of PVP and cobalt nitrate. From TEM image shown in Fig. 3c, the belts are composed of nanoparticles and some pores are observed. The HR-TEM image (Fig. 3d) shows continuous parallel lattice fringes and the lattice spacings are ca. 0.24nm and0.29nm, which are consistent with the { 311 } and { 220 } interplanar spacings of Co3O4. The dihedral angle is measured to be ca. 65 ◦ , which is consistent with the theoretical dihedral angle between (3 -1 1) plane and (220) plane. To investigate the effect of the amount of cobalt nitrate in the precursor sol, the amount of cobalt nitrate was varied with other conditions constant. As shown in Fig. 4a1, b1, c1, decreasing the amount of cobalt nitrate did not changing the belt-like morphology of the obtained gel fiber, but increasing its amount to0.58g, the prepared precursor sol was not spinnable for its inappropriate viscosity. As the amount of cobalt nitrate increased from0.115g to0.23g and0.45g, all the obtained gel fibers present belt-like morphology while their width increased. The corresponding calcined fibers are denonted as F1, F2and F3. From the XRD patterns (Fig. S1), the diffraction peaks of the three calcined samples are ascribed to cubic Co3O4and the grain sizes of F1, F2, and F3are calculated31.1 ± 1.1nm, 48.1 ± 1.9nm and26.9 ± 1.1nm according to Scherrer equation. From the SEM images shown in Fig. 4a2, b2and c2, all the fibers are belt-like structure and their widths are smaller than the corresponding gel fibers. Besides, with lower amount of cobalt nitrate, the belts are thinner, that is with higher organic content (PVP), the fibers shrank more seriously for the removal of the organic. By observing the corresponding TEM images, the size of nanoparticles on the fibers first increase and then decreases with the increase of the amount of cobalt nitrate, which is consistent with that calculated by XRD patterns. It is reported that the decomposition temperature of cobalt nitrate is ca. 242 ◦ C [41], so when the gel fiber was treated at high temperature, the side chains of PVP decomposed accompanying with the decomposition of cobalt nitrate. However, the main chains of PVP has not decomposed completely, so the left organic could prevent the growth of the nanoparticles at some degree. As to the lower amount of cobalt nitrate, such as0.115g or0.23g, a few of nucleations formed during the calcining process, and they are separated well by the left organics. Compared with the gel fiber containing0.23g of cobalt nitrate, the precursor fiber of F1can not supply enough atom clusters to promote the growth of the nucleation, so it tends to form nanoparticles with small size. When the amount of cobalt nitrate increased, the amount of the nucleation derived from the decomposition may increase, leading to the formation of small nanoparticles. Therefore, as to the sample F1and F2, the grain growth played an important role in the final size of nanoparticle, while nucleation affected their grain sizes for sample F2and F3. From the TEM images of the three samples (Fig. 4c1-c3), some pores are observed. In order to investigate the porosity of the samples, N2adsorptiondesorption measurement was performed. Fig. 5presents N2adsorption-desorption isotherms of the three samples and the inset is the corresponding BJH pore size distribution curve. The isotherms of the samples (Fig. 5a) show a hysteresis loop in the relative pressure range of0.4-1.0, indicating the existence of the mesoporous structure in the fibers. The adsorption isotherm belongs to a type IV curve and a type H3hysteresis loop according to Brunauer-Deming-Deming-Teller classification [42]. The BET specific surface area for F1, F2and F3is calculated to be ca.25.5, 10.5and33.8m2 /g, and the pore volume is0.097, 0.040and0.17cm3 /g respectively. The specific surface areas of the samples may be contributed by the aggregation of the nanoparticles, so mircrobelts with the smallest nanoparticles own the highest specific surface area. The corresponding BJH curve shows that the samples have a wide pore size distribution, with the concentrated pore diameter of12.11, 14.34and15.85nm. The highest specific surface area of F3may endow it with the best property among the three samples. It is reported that Co3O4could decompose H2O2into reactive hydroxyl radical ( · OH), so Co3O4exhibits intrinsic peroxidase-like activity [29-33]. During the catalytic reaction, Co3O4first breakdown H2O2to · OH, and then the highly reactive · OH oxidizes the substrate to its oxidation form. In the practical experiment, TMB and H2O2were selected as substrates to evaluate the peroxidaselike activities of the as-prepared samples. In the presence of H2O2, Co3O4could catalyze the oxidation of the substrates to develop a color change. So the peroxidase-like activity of the sample was monitored at652nm with colorimetric method. To acquire an optimal response, the effect of reaction pH, temperature and reaction time on the peroxidase-like activities of the samples were investigated (Fig. 6). The pH varied from2to12and temperature from20 ◦ C to70 ◦ C. From the relative activity shown in Fig. 6a, the optimized pH is4.0and the microbelts present catalytic activity over a wide range of pH. With the increase of reaction temperature, the peroxidase-like activities of the three samples first increase and then decrease (Fig. 6b). It is noticeable that the mirobelts exhibit high activity in a higher temperature range (>40 ◦ C) and the optimized temperatures for F1, F2, and F3are50 ◦ C, 60 ◦ C and60 ◦ C, which is higher than those reported in the literature [32,43-45]. It is reported that HRP starts to lose activity above30 ◦ C [30], whereas the as-prepared mircobelts still present high activity, demonstrating the high temperature resistance of the as-prepared samples. Therefore, the as-prepared Co3O4microbelts could be applied in a broad range in the fields of analytical biochemistry. Also the time-dependent absorbance of the oxidation product of TMB was investigated at the optimized reaction conditions (Fig. 6c). With the increase of reaction time within18min, the absorbance of the oxidation product of TMB increase, while further prolonging the reaction time, its absorbance became constant. For sample F3, it reaches maximum within6min. In the measurement, the reaction pH, temperature and reaction time were selected as4, 40 ◦ C and3min. Fig. 4. SEM and TEM images of gel fibers and calcined fibers with different amount of cobalt nitrate in the precursor sol (F1:a1, a2, a3;F2:b1, b2, b3; F3: c1, c2, c3) and the scale bars in the inset in c1-c3are50nm. Fig. 5. N2adsorption-desorption isotherms of the calcined fibers with different samples (a, F1; b, F2; c, F3). The inset in (a, b and c) are their corresponding BJH curve. Fig. 6. The relative activity curves of the three samples with different pHs (a), temperatures (b) and reaction time (c). The error bars represent the standard deviation of three measurements. Fig. 7. Michaelis relationships with TMB (a, c, e) and H2O2 (b, d, f) as substrates for F1 (a, b), F2(c, d) and F3 (e, f).
Table 1 The Michaelis constants (Km) of different samples and natural HRP.
catalystF1F2F3HRP [4]
K m (H 2 O 2 )/mM0.0790.0910.02623.7
K m (TMB)/mM0.0150.0130.0120.434
Table1The Michaelis constants (Km) of different samples and natural HRP. In order to further investigate the peroxidase-like activity of the fibers, a series of experiments were conducted by changing the concentration of one substrate with the other constant. The Michaelis constant (Km) can be obtained from the slope and the intercept of the extrapolated straight line with the horizontal axis in the Lineweaver-Burk plot. As a special constant for enzyme, Km is related to the property of enzyme, substrate or the enzymatic reaction condition (e.g. temperature, pH or inhibitor) and it represents the affinity to the substrate for a given enzyme [44]. The smaller Km value, the stronger affinity between the substrate and the enzyme is, and the strong affinity indicates that at a low substrate concentration the reaction velocity would reach maximum. By changing the concentrations of the substrate (TMB and H2O2), the absorbance was measured within the same time and the corresponding Lineweaver-Burk plots are shown in Fig. 7. The Km values for F1, F2, and F3towards different substrates are listed in Table1. From the table, The Km value of F3was the smallest among the three samples with TMB and H2O2as the substrates, demonstrating that F3presents strongest affinity to TMB and H2O2. This might due to the largest surface area of F3and strongest adsorption ability of TMB. Therefore, F3presents highest peroxidase-like activity. Compared with HRP, whose Km(TMB) and Km(H2O2) values are3.7mM and0.434mM [1,4], those for the three sample are small, indicating their perxoidase-activity can reach a maximal velocity at a low concentration of TMB or H2O2. At the same reaction conditions, the peroxidase-like activity of the three samples (F1, F2and F3) was tested. As shown in Fig. 8, without catalyst, TMB was nearly not oxidized by H2O2, so this contribution to absorbance can be ignored. Among the three samples, F3exhibits the best activity, and the activities of the three samples follow the order: F3 > F1 > F2 .The activity of F1is57.2% of that of F3, and for F2, its activity is only34.8% of F3. The activity order is the same as their BET surface area. The best activity of F3mainly results from the smallest nanoparticles, highest specific surface area and strongest affinity to TMB and H2O2. The peroxidase-like activity of Co3O4microbelts may originate from their catalytic decomposition of H2O2into reactive hydroxyl radical ( · OH). In order to verify the catalytic mechanism, ESR spintrapping technique is employed, which is the most direct method Fig. 8. The relative activity of three samples at the optimized conditions. Fig. 9. ESR spectra of different samples and that without catalyst named Blank. to detect · OH. In the measurement, DMPO was used as a spin trap. Results of the three samples are shown in Fig. 9. From the spectra, in the presence of the samples, four lines with intensity ratio of1:2:2:1were detected, which is the characteristic ESR signal of nitroxide radical generated upon reaction between · OH and DMPO [46]. In addition, there are some differences in the line intensity of the samples, which may result from the amount of the generated · OH. The line intensity order is coincident with that of the peroxidase-like activity, indicating that the activity depends on the ability to generate · OH and the more · OH formed, the higher the activity. While without catalyst whose spectrum is denoted as Blank, no obvious signal appears, demonstrating that the as-prepared Co3O4microbelts exhibits catalytic activity for decomposing H2O2into · OH. From above analysis, it is speculated that in the catalytic reaction, Co3O4first breakdown H2O2to · OH, and then the highly reactive · OH oxidizes the substrate to its oxidation form. Therefore, Co3O4presents intrinsic peroxidase-like activity. As an inorganic material derived from calcination, Co3O4fibers, may exhibit high chemical and thermal stability, so the recycling experiments were performed and the peroxidise-like activity was examined in each cycle. As presented in Fig. S2, the peroxidise-like activities of sample F1, F2, and F3is41.9%, 59.6% and78.7% of the corresponding initial activities after ten recycles. The decrease of their activities mainly results from the loss of the catalyst [31,44]. The microscale fibers less easily lose than the nanofibers, so the microbelt (F2and F3) exhibits the better stability than F1. While the bigger nanopartilces in the microscaled fibers more easily lose than the smaller ones, so the stability of F3is higher than F2. Therefore, the microbelts composed by small nanoparticles exhibit superior stability. # 4. Conclusions We have successfully prepared1D Co3O4mircobelts with electrospinning technique. The Co3O4nanostructure presents superior peroxidase-like activity in a wide range of pH and temperature. The as-prepared samples showed good affinity to H2O2and TMB, and the microbelts with higher surface area presents better peroxidaselike activity and stability. It is believed that Co3O4microbelts may be applied as an artificial peroxidase for potential fields. # Acknowledgments This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 21401114and21303091), Project of university innovation of Jinan (Grant No. J14LC02) and the Promotive Research Fundation for Excellent Young and Middleaged Scientisits of Shandong Province (BS2013CL002). # Appendix A.
